courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Introduction to Robot Design
courses.description: The course covers introduction to robots in different industries, microcontroller unit, programming using Arduino, sensors, actuators, mechanisms and engineering design principles. It provides the students extensive hands-on training on robot prototype development and experimental evaluation. It adopts a project-based learning approach: the students are required to design and build a fully functional robotic system that must be able to perform a specific task. Both mechanical and electronic hardware will be designed and developed by the students. The lectures and laboratory sessions are designed so as to equip them with the essential knowledge to successfully complete their project.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
(1) Arduino Programming Notebook. B. Evans, 1st edition, 2007
(2) Autonomous Mobile Robots. R. Siegwart and I.R. Nourbakhsh, MIT Press, 2004
(3) Red G. Martin, Robotic Explorations: A Hands-on Introduction to Engineering, Prentice Hall, 2001

courses.description: This course introduces engineering design fundamentals, manufacturing, and practical use of advanced computer-aided design software and prototyping techniques. The topics include engineering graphics, geometric dimensioning and tolerances, working drawings, conventional manufacturing, reverse engineering, digital manufacturing (CNC), additive manufacturing, and micromachining.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Engineering Mechanics
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to the statics and dynamics. You will be introduced to and become familiar with all relevant physical properties and fundamental laws governing the behavior of particles and rigid bodies, and you will learn how to solve a variety of problems in terms of kinematics, kinetics, energy, and momentum of particles and rigid bodies. The emphasis is on the physical understanding of why particles or rigid bodies behave the way they do in both the statics and the dynamics.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESTR2400.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
(1) R. C. Hibbeler, et al. Engineering Mechanics Statics in SI unit, 14th Edition, 2009.
(2) R. C. Hibbeler, et al. Engineering Mechanics Dynamics in SI unit, 14th Edition, 2009.
(3) F. P. Beer et al. Vector Mechanics for Engineers: Statics, 9th Edition, 2009.
(4) F. P. Beer et al. Vector Mechanics for Engineers: Dynamics, 9th Edition, 2009.
(5) James L. Meriam, L. G. Kraige, J. N. Bolton, Engineering Mechanics: Statics, 8th Edition, Wiley
(6) James L. Meriam, L. G. Kraige, J. N. Bolton, Engineering Mechanics: Dynamics, 8th Edition, Wiley

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Thermodynamics
courses.description: This course will cover fundamental topics in thermodynamics including basic concepts, heat and work, pure substance, ideal gas, first law and second law of thermodynamics, entropy, gas and vapor power cycles, and refrigeration cycles.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESTR2402.

courses.recommended_readings: Yunus A. Cengel and Michael A. Boles, Thermodynamics, An Engineering Approach, 8th Edition in SI units, McGraw-Hill.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Robot Development in Practice: From Design to Prototyping
courses.description: This course is aimed to enhance students' professional and engineering interests through practicum and team-based development of practical robots through participation in hands-on competitions. This is a project-based experiential course that exposes students to design and build practical robots. Students will be required to conceptualize and perform high-level design of a robot system, and then design and build various components using skills including CAD drawing, mechanical structure, electronic circuits and programming skills. Students will practice their engineering knowledge through tutorial-style workshops and laboratory sessions. The goal is to develop and nurture skills in problem-solving, hands-on skills, project and time management through the project. Students should obtain approval from the course instructor to ensure that the participated activities are in-line with the course learning outcomes.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Technology, Society and Engineering Practice
courses.description: This course introduces basic and fundamental knowledge in technology, engineering, and their impacts on human society. Important and various aspects of engineering will be introduced, like engineering design and innovation, engineering project management, and intellectual property. Student have opportunities to participate some field study to have a better understanding of engineering design, innovation, and industrial manufacturing in practice. This course also provides several seminars given by real engineers with topics on current engineering issues, career decision-making strategies, and opinions such as internships and life-long study.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ENGG2601.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

(2) A. Shtub et al., Project management: processes, methodologies, and economics, 2nd edition, Pearson Prentice Hall, 2005.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Mechanics of Materials

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 803

courses.description: This course is a study of mechanics of materials. It covers the following topics: linear elasticity, stress and strain, stress-strain relations, loading and deformation, statically indeterminate problems, torsion, shear forces and bending moments, stresses in beams, deflection of beams, stresses in thin-walled pressure vessels, column and buckling.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

(2) Mechanics of Materials, by J. M. Gere and B. J. Goodno, Brief Edition, Cengage Learning, 2011.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Manufacturing Technology
courses.description: This course provides students with fundamental background of manufacturing technologies. It covers the following topics: overview of manufacturing technologies, engineering materials, metal forming processes, plastic forming processes, 3D printing processes, machining processes, micro-manufacturing and fabrication. A number of industrial visits and hands-on labs are also included.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESTR3404.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings: Mikell P. Groover, Fundamentals of modern manufacturing: materials, processes, and systems, Wiley Publisher.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Fluid Mechanics
courses.description: This course covers important topics in fluid mechanics. It will introduce the nature of fluids; pressure, hydrostatics, and buoyancy; integral and differential equations of fluid flows; conservation of mass, momentum, and energy; basic mass transfer; inviscid flow, viscous-dominated flows; Navier-Stokes equation; dimensional analysis; internal and external flows, boundary layers, and lift and drag on objects.
courses.enrollment_requirement: (ENR) PR-MAEG2030/ESTR2402/Consent of the course instructor.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings: The recommended reading list/references will be determined by course instructor.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Mechanical Design
courses.description: This course covers the engineering design principles, machine design methods, design of machine components, and kinematics. Load analysis, design for manufacturing, reliability and human factors will also be introduced and reviewed.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by the students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings: Richard G. Budynas. Shigley's Mechanical Engineering Design. New York, NY: McGraw-Hill Science/Engineering/Math: 2014, 10 edition.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Introduction to Control Systems
courses.description: This course provides students with fundamental knowledge of control systems. It covers the following topics: mathematical modelling and linear approximation of engineering systems, Laplace transform, transfer function and block diagram representation, characteristics of feedback systems, performance specifications, Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion, root locus design, frequency response design and Nyquist criterion. The utilization of computer-aided analysis and design software is also included.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESTR3406.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.

(2) Feedback Control of Dynamic Systems, by G.F. Franklin, J.D. Powell, and A. Emami-Naeini, 7th Edition, Prentice Hall.
(3) Modern Control Engineering, by K. Ogata, 5th Edition, Prentice Hall.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Introduction to Robotics
courses.description: The course covers the basic and fundamentals to the study of robotic manipulators, including: classification of robots, robot sensors and actuators, coordinate frames and homogeneous transformations, Denavit-Hartenberg notation, forwards and inverse kinematics, Jacobians and statics, singularity and workspace analysis, and trajectory generation. The course includes a creative project component for students to apply robot manipulators for a new and innovative application. This requires the students to design a new application for robot manipulators that would be useful to the community and globally. By using the introduction to the state-of-the-art in robotics around the world, and fundamentals learnt in the course, the students have to innovatively design and present the idea as a mock start-up company project.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESTR3408 or ELEG3103.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
(1) John J. Craig: Introduction to Robotics, 3rd Edition, Prentice Hall, 2005.
(2) Bruno Siciliano et. Al.: Robotics: Modelling Planning and Control, Springer, 2009

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Fundamentals of Computer-Aided Design
courses.description: This course provides students with fundamentals of Computer Aided Design. It covers the following topics: elements of interactive graphics in CAD/CAM, mathematical bases and manipulation of curves and surfaces (parametric cubic curve, Bezier and NURBS curve, ruled surface, sweep surface, Coon's bilinear surface, Hermite surface, Bezier and NURBS surfaces), introduction to geometric and solid modeling (constructive solid geometry, boundary representation), and visualization for engineering simulation. Applications in design and manufacturing are also included.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

(2) Edward Angel, Interactive Computer Graphics, A Top-Down Approach with OpenGL, Addison Wesley, 2000.
(3) http://www.opengl.org/

courses.title: Fundamentals of Machine Intelligence

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 703

William M W Mong Eng Bldg 703
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 703

courses.description: This course provides students with fundamental knowledge of machine intelligence. It covers the following topics: data structures, sorting and searching, knowledge representation (state space, logical statements, rules, connectionism), discrete problem solving by state space search, deduction by resolution in predicate logic, inference by ruled-based systems, mappings by networks, and principles of learning. The application examples are also included.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Engineering Design and Applications

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404

courses.description: The course is intended to enable students to learn the fundamentals of engineering design and how to carry out the design process. One (or several) engineering project(s)s for students to practice is (are) included with the following topics: engineering design process, innovation and design basics, CAD and CAE tools and applications, prototyping (mechanical workshop), prototyping (electronics workshop), quality and inspection.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students should have achieved the following outcomes:
(1) understand the design and implementation of engineering design technologies to address specific engineering challenges during and after the course.
(2) be skilled at communicating with appropriate software and media, including mathematical graphical representations (engineering 2D and 3D drawings), written reports and oral presentations for an assigned project.
(3) understand the strategic aspects and processes of new product design by applying computer integrated manufacturing technology, such as rapid prototyping and 3D printing.
(4) be capable of introducing the influences of technological, environmental, social and cultural considerations on design and manufacturing decisions.
(5) be capable of performing evaluation and identification of the target markets and customers, strategic product features and benefits of the new designs and manufacturing methods.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
(1) Engineering Design: A Project-Based Introduction, by Clive L. Dym, Patrick Little, Elizabeth J. Orwin and R. Erik Spjut, 3rd edition, Wiley 2009
(2) Engineering Design, by Rudolph J. Eggert, Pearson Prentice Hall 2005.

courses.description: This course provides students with a solid background in the fundamentals of computer-integrated manufacturing technology. It covers the following topics: concurrent engineering, computer-integrated manufacturing models and concepts, rapid prototyping, computer-aided manufacturing, control of manufacturing systems (numerical control and computer numerical control), programmable logic controller, computer aided process planning and manufacturing scheduling, and quality assurance. The hands-on experiments and projects are included.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Finite Element Modelling and Analysis
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to learn the mathematical and computational method for finite element analysis. Students will learn the computational procedure, basic elements, shape functions and the method for imposing boundary conditions. The numerical implementation for finite element analysis will also be learned. Finite element analysis software will be used in project to solve engineering applications.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Heat Transfer
courses.description: This course will cover fundamental topics in heat transfer including physical origins of three heat transfer modes and their rate equations, heat diffusion equation, boundary and initial conditions, steady-state and transient conduction, numerical methods, boundary layer equation, external and internal forced convection, free convection, and thermal radiation.

Not for students who have taken ESTR4412.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Mechatronic Systems

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404

courses.description: Mechatronics is at the intersection of mechanical, electronic, control, and software engineering. This course will cover the technologies involved in developing and designing intelligent electro-mechanical systems. Specific topics include physical system modelling and analysis, measurement and manipulation principles, sensors, actuators, data acquisition and conversion, microcontrollers and interface, control system design and tuning. The course includes a significant lab component with a course project in mechatronics system design. Students will involve in hands-on programming project to reinforce the basic principles developed in lectures as well as develop control algorithm implementation skillsets.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Modern Control Systems Analysis and Design
courses.description: This course provides students with the knowledge of modern control systems analysis and design. It covers the following topics: continuous and discrete domain state space representations (including transition matrix, stability, controllability and observability, pole placement control, state estimator), emulation design, and discrete design. Case studies are also included.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Introduction to Combustion

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 703
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 703
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 703
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 804
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 804
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 804
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 804

courses.description: Combustion and its control are essential to our daily life and more than 80% of the energy used nowadays is from combustion sources. This course starts with the basic principles that govern combustion phenomena such as conservation of mass, the first and second laws of thermodynamics, and the momentum principle. Then students will learn the new applications of these basic principles to account for the chemical transformations. We will discuss the basic concepts of chemical kinetics and the forms of reaction rate laws that apply in combustion processes, as well as mass transport phenomena. These laws will be applied to a variety of combustion phenomena, including spontaneous ignition of combustible mixtures, pollutant formation, internal combustion engine and fire safety.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Pre-requisite: MAEG2030/ESTR2402 and MAEG3030.
Not for students who have taken ESTR4420.

(2) John B. Heywood, “Internal Combustion Engine Fundamentals”, April 1988, McGraw-Hill Companies,Inc. (ISBN: 007028637X)
(3) Sara McAllister, Jyh-Yuan Chen, A. Carlos Fernandez-Pello, “Fundamentals of Combustion Processes”, April 2011, Springer-Verlag New York, LLC. (ISBN: 1441979425)

courses.description: The course is designed to provide students with an opportunity to carry out, under the supervision of an academic staff, an independent project with research elements in engineering.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Geometric Computing for Design and Manufacturing
courses.description: Geometric computing tools have been widely used in modern product design and realization, such as all kinds of Computer-Aided Design (CAD), Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM) and Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE) software systems. However, the capability of simply using these CAD, CAM and CAE software systems is not sufficient for future products design and manufacturing. This course aims to help students in understanding the principles of geometric computing behind CAD, CAM and CAE systems, and provides students with deep understanding of computational techniques and practical experience in developing novel computational design and manufacturing applications.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or For students in UG Mechanical and Automation Engineering;Pre-requisite: MAEG3070 (for UG students only)

2. Mortenson, M. E. Geometric Modeling. Wiley Computer Publishing, 1997.
3. M. de Berg, et. al. Computational Geometry – Algorithms and Applications. Springer, 2000.
4. Shah, J. and M. Mantyla, Parametric and Feature-Based CAD/CAM, John Wiley and Sons, 1995.
5. Hoffmann, C. M. Geometric and Solid Modeling. Morgan Kaufman Publishers, 1989.

courses.title: Smart Materials and Structures

meetings.location: Fung King Hey Bldg Swire Hall1
Fung King Hey Bldg Swire Hall1

courses.description: The contents of this course include overview of smart materials technology, characteristics of smart materials such as piezoelectric materials, magnetorheological fluids, and shape memory alloys. It covers smart actuators and sensors; structural modelling and design; dynamics and control for smart structures; integrated system analysis; and applications in biomedical devices, precision machinery, transportation, and buildings.

(2) Journal of Intelligent Material Systems and Structures, Sage Publications

(4) Engineering Analysis of Smart Material Systems, by D. J. Leo, John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 2007
(5) Adaptronics and Smart Structures: Basics, Materials, Design, and Applications, by H. Janocha (ed.), Springer-Verlag, 2nd edition, 2007
(6) Adaptive Structures: Dynamics and Control, by R. L. Clark, W. R. Saunders, and G. P. Gibbs, John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 1998

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Topics in Robotics

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg LT
William M W Mong Eng Bldg LT

courses.description: This course aims to teach students a range of classical and state-of-the-art topics through a series of examples. The focus will be on how different fundamental topics, such as linear and non-linear control, optimization, path planning, visual servo control, robot kinematics and dynamics, and machine learning, are applied through practical applications within robotics. Different application scenarios that may be used to show different fundamental topics include: mobile manipulation, bio-inspired and humanoid robots, robotic walking, rehabilitation robotics, medical and surgical robots, cable-driven robots, and autonomous ground, water and aerial vehicles.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or For students in UG Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

courses.recommended_readings:
(1) Springer Handbook of Robotics, Editors: Bruno Siciliano, Oussama Khatib, 2016
(2) Research papers and references to be provided by course teacher.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology: Fundamentals and Applications
courses.description: This course provides both fundamental knowledge of nanomaterials and nanotechnology and advanced topics related to applications. These topics cover basic principles, which include the scaling law, the surface science for nanomaterials, observation and characterization tools for nanomaterials, the nanofabrication techniques, building blocks for nanodevices and systems, etc. In the second half of this course, advanced topics on applying nanomaterials and nanotechnology for applications in mechanical engineering, energy engineering and biomedical engineering will be covered.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc/MPhil-PhD/UG Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or UG Energy and Environmental Engineering;Pre-requisite (for UG students only): [EEEN3030 or ESTR3402 (with grade B or above)] or [with the consent of the course instructor]

(2) Fundamentals of Microfabrication and Nanotechnology, Third Edition, Three-Volume Set (3 Book Series), by Marc Madou, CRC Press, ISBN: ISBN 9780849331800
(3) Springer Handbook of Nanotechnology (3rd), Ed. Bharat Bhushan, Springer, ISBN: 978-3-642-02524-2
(4) Fundamentals and Applications of Nanomaterials, Artech House, ISBN: 1596932627

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Computational Mechanics
courses.description: Mechanics is the foundation of many emerging research and engineering topics. With the rapid advancement in computing power, numerical methods are preferred to solve differential equations governing the physical process. It opens a whole new domain in industrial design, manufacturing process analysis, material behaviour prediction, etc. This course covers theoretical fundamentals in computational mechanics, including continuum mechanics, finite element methods, and computational plasticity. In addition, the course will also introduce practical skills to applying computational mechanics in research, including multi-physics simulation and advanced finite element simulation techniques.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or For students in UG Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
(1) F. Dunne, N. Petrinic, Introduction to Computational Plasticity, Oxford University Press, 2005.
(2) Belytschko, T., Liu, W.K., Moran, B. and Elkhodary, K., 2013. Nonlinear finite elements for continua and structures. John wiley & sons.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Materials Characterization Techniques

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg LT

courses.description: This course focuses on a suite of materials characterization techniques that are useful in energy and environmental sciences. The main targets of these techniques include functional materials that are used in energy and environmental applications as well as solid, liquid, and gas samples that are involved in energy production and conversion, and pollution monitoring and control. The techniques include mass spectrometry (MS), gas chromatography (GC), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), infrared (IR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), electron microscopy, and X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy. Students will receive lectures on the theory and operation principle of each technique as well as its limitations, and obtain hands-on experience with some of the techniques in supplemental lab sessions.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD/MSc/UG Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or UG Energy and Environmental Engineering;Pre-requisite (for UG students only): EEEN3030 or ESTR3402 (with grade B or above)
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings: (1) Chapter 4, "Concepts of modern catalysis and kinetics" by I. Chorkendorff and J.W. Niemantsverdriet, Weinheim: Wiley-VCH, 2007.

courses.title: Advanced Heat Transfer and Fluid Mechanics

meetings.instructor:

courses.description: This course will cover advanced topics in heat transfer and fluid mechanics including overview of macroscopic theory of heat transfer, microscopic picture of heat carriers and their transport, micro- and nanoscale energy transport in solids, chemical thermodynamics, chemical kinetics, multicomponent and multiphase mixtures, basic principles of computational fluid dynamics, turbulence modeling, and airflow simulation in enclosed environments.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Design for Additive Manufacturing
courses.description: This course provides a theoretical and practical guidance on how to design parts to gain the maximum benefit from what additive manufacturing (AM) can offer. It begins by describing the main AM technologies and their respective advantages and disadvantages. It then examines strategic considerations in the context of designing for additive manufacturing (DfAM), such as designing to avoid anisotropy, designing to minimize print time, and post-processing, before discussing the economics of AM. The course then focuses on computational tools for design analysis and the optimization of AM parts, part consolidation, and tooling applications. Both designing for polymer AM and metal AM and its corresponding design guidelines will be provided. The main benefit of the course is its combined theoretical and practical approach, which provides directly applicable, “hands-on” information and insights to help students adopt AM in their daily practice.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering; or For students in UG Mechanical and Automation Engineering - Major

(2) M.P. Bendsoe, Topological Optimization. Theory, Methods and Applications, Springer, 2004
(3) Topology Optimization in Engineering Structure Design, ISTE-Elsevier, 2017

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Geometric Modelling for Computer Aided Design and Manufacturing
courses.description: This course focuses on the technologies for the modeling and visualization of 3D objects. This includes topics on the rendering of 3D objects, and the mathematical descriptions of curves and surfaces commonly used in Computer-Aided Design and Manufacturing systems. The basic theory of solid modeling and solid representation scheme will be covered. Some popular solid representation schemes and the algorithms for Boolean operations on solid models will also be covered. The basic concept for the generation of tool paths from 3D models for machining and 3D printing will be studied.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering;Not for students who have taken MAEG5030

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) Technology
courses.description: This course will be offered to cover the knowledge of Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) technologies, including sensors, actuators, signal processing, AI control and simulation. To support the adoption of Internet of Things (IoT) technologies for human-computer interface applications, system design, communication, system model, control theory and AI analytics will be taught to make an integrated interaction system with computer analytics and simulation capability.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students should have achieved the following outcomes:

1. Understand sensors and sensor interface for automated systems;
2. Understand actuators and control forhuman-computer interfaces and teleoperated systems; and
3. Understand system models, signal processing, communication, control theory and AI analytics for different computer interaction applications.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

1. Grigore C. Burdea and O. Colffet, Virtual Reality Technology, John Wiley & Sons Inc., 1994
2. Grigore C. Burdea, Force and Touch Feedback for Virtual Reality, John Wiley & Sons Inc., 1996
3. Donald Hearn and M. Pauline Baker, Computer Graphics, C version, 2nd Editon, Prentice Hall, 1997.
4. Research Papers posted on the course website

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Computer Vision in Practice
courses.description: This course provides a hands-on introduction to computer vision, focusing on practical applications and real-world challenges. Students will explore key concepts such as image processing, feature extraction, object detection, segmentation, and deep learning-based vision models, etc. Through coding assignments and projects, students will implement and optimize vision algorithms. The course emphasizes practical problem-solving, deployment considerations, and the integration of vision systems into real-world applications.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering

courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Control and Industrial Automation

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg LT
William M W Mong Eng Bldg LT
William M W Mong Eng Bldg LT

courses.description: This course is designed to equip students with the knowledge of modern control systems analysis and design, and skills of control theory for practical applications of industrial automation systems. It will cover the following topics: state space representation, realizability, stability, controllability, observability; linear control design methods including pole placement, observer, asymptotic tracking and disturbance rejection, internal model design, and feedforward design; introduction to nonlinear systems. Various examples, e.g., robot control, satellite's attitude control and servomechanism are included.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering;Not for students who have taken ENGG5403
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students should have achieved the following outcomes:
1. Able to derive a state-space model for a given physical system;
2. Able to perform stability analysis and computer simulation of time responses of the model; and
3. Able to perform feedback controller design for fulfilling a given specification.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. Feedback control of dynamic systems, 6ed, Gene F. Franklin, Prentice Hall, 2012
2. Applied Nonlinear Control, J.J. Slotine and W. Li, Prentice Hall Englewood Cliffs, 1991

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Applied Computational Intelligence
courses.description: This course applies artificial intelligent algorithms to solve mechanical and automation problems. It consists of the following topics: various areas of emerging technologies of artificial intelligent systems; introduction and review of neural networks; fuzzy systems; simulated annealing; swarm intelligence and genetic algorithms. The applications of intelligent systems to control, robotics, automation, manufacturing, and transportation systems.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students should have achieved the following outcomes:

1. Understand the emerging technologies of various human-inspired or nature-inspired artificial intelligent systems;
2. Understand the functions and characteristics of specific human-inspired or nature-inspired artificial intelligent systems; and

3. Understand the design principles and application potentials of human-inspired or nature-inspired

artificial intelligent systems.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. R. Rojas, Neural Networks: A Systematic Introduction, Springer, 1996.
2. J. M. Zurada, Introduction to Artificial Neural Systems, West, 1992.
3. S. Haykin, Neural Networks: A Comprehensive Foundation (2nd Ed), Prentice-Hall, 1999.
4. C.-T. Lin and C.S. G. Lee, Neural Fuzzy Systems, Prentice Hall, 1996.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Mechatronic Systems
courses.description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of mechatronic systems, emphasizing the integration of mechanical, electronic, and computer engineering principles. Students will engage in practical projects to design, build, and optimize mechatronic systems, incorporating advanced techniques such as AI and computational intelligence. The course covers physical system modeling, sensors and actuators, data acquisition, microcontrollers, control system design, and system integration. Through case studies and hands-on projects, students will develop the skills necessary to innovate and lead in the field of mechatronics.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.course_syllabus:
* Physical System Modeling and Analysis
* Measurement and Manipulation Principles
* Sensors and Actuators
* Data Acquisition and Conversion
* Microcontrollers and Interface
* Control System Design and Tuning
* AI Techniques in Mechatronics
* Case Studies in System Integration
* Course Project, Project Management and Teamwork
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. Introduction to Mechatronics and Measurement Systems, by David G. Alciatore and Michael B. Histand (5th Edition), McGraw-Hill, ISBN: 9781259892349 or 9781260816440c2012.
2. Handbook of AI-Based Mechatronics Systems and Smart Solutions in Industrial Automation (1st Edition, ISBN 9781032614359); Edited by Pushpalatha Naveenkumar, Vandana Sharma, Gunapriya D., Azween Abdullah, and Ahmed A. Elngar, CRC Press, 2025.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Robotics
courses.description: This course will first give an introduction to robotics and its applications in industries and services; and then teach fundamentals in robotics including kinematics and dynamics of robot manipulators, sensors and actuators, motion control, and machine intelligence. Finally, advanced modern topics related to the research and application development of robotics will be introduced.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering;Not for students who have taken MAEG5090 or ENGG5402 or ROSE5710
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. Introduction to Robotics: Mechanics and Control, 4th edition, John J. Craig, Addison Wesley Publishing Co. Ltd. 2018
2. Control of Robot Manipulators, F. L. Lewis, C. T. Abdallah, and D. A. Dawson, Macmillan Publishing Company, 1993.
3. Modern robotics: Mechanics, Planning and Control, KevinN. Lynch and Frank Park, Cambridge University Press, 2017
4. In-class Handouts

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Smart Materials and Structures
courses.description: The contents of this course include overview of smart materials technology, characteristics of smart materials such as piezoelectric materials, magnetorheological fluids, and shape memory alloys. It covers smart actuators and sensors; structural modelling and design; dynamics and control for smart structures; integrated system analysis; and applications in buildings, automobiles, trains, robots, manufacturing systems, and medical devices.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Energy and Environment
courses.description: This course will discuss the topics of energy and environment from the perspective of mechanical engineering. The topics include global energy consumption and supply, thermodynamic principles of energy conversion and efficiency, fossil fuel usage, operation of nuclear power plant, emerging technologies utilizing renewable energy sources, energy harvesting and storage technologies, environmental impacts, energy systems management and government policy.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: The course will be evaluated by course evaluation done by students.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings: 1. Fundamentals of renewable energy processes, 2ed

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Precision Machine Design
courses.description: This interdisciplinary course is designed to address the challenges of modern precision machine design and high-speed automation technologies. It covers machine design principles, precision metrology, precision machining, vibration control, and instrumentation. Students will gain industrial knowledge from the photonics, semiconductor, and biotechnology sectors, along with hands-on experience and practical skills to design, instrument, and control precision machinery and automation systems.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.learning_outcomes: After completing this course, students should understand the fundamental principles of precision machine design and the key technologies involved in high-precision mechanical components (e.g., actuators, sensors, and transmission elements), and be able to design, analyze, and implement precision systems. The course develops professional engineering competencies that enable students to pursue career opportunities in relevant industries.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.

2. Basics of precision engineering - Edited by Richard Leach & Stuart T. Smith; CRC Press; 2018

3. Mechanical vibrations - S. S. Rao; Pearson (6th edition in SI units); 2017

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: M.Sc. Project
courses.description: In this course, each student will perform research on a topic related to research areas in the Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering. The project will be supervised by faculty members in the MAE department. At the end of the project, the student is required to submit a written report and give an oral presentation for the assessment of performance.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.course_syllabus: In this course, each student will perform research on a topic related to research areas in the MAE department under the supervision of faculty members. No class will be arranged. The student will meet with the supervisor regularly to discuss the research progress. At the end of the project, the student is required to submit a written report and give an oral presentation for the assessment of performance.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. Email feedback
2. Review meeting
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings: Reading materials will be provided by each supervisor according to research topics.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Mechanical and Automation Engineering Internship
courses.description:
This course is designed to allow students to acquire a basic understanding and the skills of the practical aspects of the Mechanical and Automation Engineering profession. During the internship, the student must attach to a company in a study-related position for no less than 12 weeks. The student will have an academic supervisor (as primary supervisor) and an industry co-supervisor from the company, both have the expertise to provide advice to the student. To be qualified for award of the subject credit, the student must submit a report summarizing the internship experience at the end of the internship. Additional presentation may be required by the hosting company.

The internship should normally take place in the summer term after a student has finished the first two semesters of studies. Part-time students can decide to undertake the internship in the summer term of either the first or second year of studies.

Students are recommended to seek the Professor-in-Charge's comment on potential internship opportunities before enrolling in the course.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Course evaluation by students.
courses.required_readings: Readings will be recommended by the Professor-in-charge.
courses.recommended_readings: Readings will be recommended by the Professor-in-charge.

courses.subject: MAEG
courses.title: Thesis Research
courses.description: In this course, a student is required to meet with his/her supervisor regularly who provides necessary guidance and supervision to write up a thesis and monitors the student's academic progress.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Mechanical and Automation Engineering
courses.learning_outcomes: Under the regular supervision of the thesis advisor, the student learns how to formulate a research question in an area of his/her interest, articulate the long-term goals and shorter-term objectives of his/her research, critically review the background literatures, plan and conduct the research activities, become competent in the underpinning knowledge and skills, analyze, present and discuss the results thoroughly, and draw appropriate conclusions based on the findings. The student is expected to mature as a more independent researcher as he/she progresses in his/her research study.
courses.course_syllabus: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. Discussion with students.
2. Email and face-to-face feedback.
courses.required_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
courses.recommended_readings: The details will be provided by the course instructor.
